title: Dyeing with onion skins
subtitle: A reliable beginner dye giving strong gold and bronze on alum-mordanted wool
excerpt: Onion skins are one of the most effective and accessible natural dyes. Yellow onion skins give a warm gold to bronze on alum-mordanted wool; red onion skins give a muted olive-green. The dye is strong enough to need no pre-soaking and is one of the few natural dyes that gives reasonable colour without a mordant at all, though mordanted fibre takes a much deeper result.
sourceNotes: Onion skin as a natural dye is documented in dyeing texts including Jenny Dean's 'Wild Colour'. Yellow onion skins contain quercetin and kaempferol flavonoids that bond well to protein fibres. Observation-led.

glossaryTerms.definition: The water in which dye plant material has been simmered to extract colour. The mordanted fibre is added to the cooled or warm dye bath to take up the colour.
Fibre that has been pre-treated with a mordant (typically alum) to improve dye uptake and wash fastness. Mordanted fibre produces deeper, more lasting colour than unmordanted fibre.

The process of running multiple batches through a dye bath, with each batch taking progressively lighter colour as the dye is used up. A gradient from deep gold to pale straw can be achieved by exhausting an onion skin bath over three or four batches.

content.text: Yellow onion skins give a dependable warm gold on alum-

wool, ranging from bright gold with cream of tartar to a deeper bronze without it. Collect the dry outer skins of yellow onions (not the flesh): you need approximately 100 g of dry skins to dye 100 g of fibre to a medium depth. Onion skins dry and store well, so they can be accumulated from cooking over several months. They are not classified as dye plants requiring specialist sourcing.

attrs.title: Mordant safety
attrs.body: Wear dedicated gloves, work in a ventilated space, and keep mordant jars away from food and children.

items.name: Dry yellow onion skins
items.qty: 100 g (equal weight to fibre for medium depth)

items.name: Stainless steel dye pot, minimum 4 litre
Strainer or muslin bag (for the spent skins)

content.text: Place the dry onion skins in the dye pot with enough cold water to allow the fibre to move freely later. Bring slowly to a simmer (80-90°C) and hold for 30-40 minutes until the water is a deep amber-brown. Strain out the spent skins through a strainer or muslin bag and discard them. Allow the
to cool to around 60°C before entering the fibre.

Lower the pre-wetted, mordanted fibre into the warm dye bath. Raise the temperature slowly to 80-85°C over 30 minutes, stirring gently. Maintain that temperature for 45-60 minutes. The fibre will take on colour quickly in the first 10 minutes; the last 30 minutes deepen and even the colour.

Allow the pot to cool to 40°C. Lift the fibre gently and rinse in water at the same temperature until the rinse water runs clear. The first rinse will typically be faintly coloured: this is surface dye that was not fully bonded. Press out excess water and dry the fibre in shade to preserve colour depth.
Exhausting the bath for lighter shades

well. After the first batch, the remaining bath gives a lighter gold on the next batch, then a pale straw. Running three batches from one extraction gives a natural gradient palette from deep amber to pale gold.

attrs.heading: Common problems
attrs.intro: Two problems in onion skin dyeing.

items.symptom: Colour is very pale despite good mordanting
items.cause: The onion skins were not dry enough, reducing the concentration of flavonoid pigments in the extraction.
items.fix: Use only fully dried skins. Fresh onion skin contains mostly water and gives weak colour. Store skins in a paper bag in a dry place until they are papery and fully desiccated.

items.symptom: Colour is uneven or patchy on the yarn
items.cause: The dye bath was too hot when the fibre entered, or the fibre was not stirred often enough during the dye stage.
items.fix: Always enter fibre into a warm (not hot) bath and raise temperature slowly. Stir every 5 minutes to expose all parts of the fibre to the bath equally.
